article.title: Cisco Moves to Acquire Astrix Security in Reported $400 Million Deal to Police Non-Human Identities and AI Agents
article.summary: The May 4, 2026 announcement folds Astrix's discovery, governance, and threat-detection tooling for API keys, service accounts, and agentic identities into Cisco Identity Intelligence, Secure Access, and Duo IAM.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Cisco announced on May 4, 2026 its intent to acquire Astrix Security, an Israeli startup specializing in non-human identity (NHI) security, [according to SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/04/cisco-buys-astrix-security-strengthen-ai-agent-discovery-governance/). Financial terms were not officially disclosed by Cisco, but [Calcalist reported](https://www.calcalistech.com/ctechnews/article/dy5obf581) the deal value at approximately $400 million, while [SiliconANGLE noted](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/04/cisco-buys-astrix-security-strengthen-ai-agent-discovery-governance/) that The Information had previously speculated a figure closer to $300 million. The acquisition adds Astrix's tooling for discovering, governing, and securing API keys, service accounts, OAuth tokens, and AI agent identities to Cisco's expanding security platform.

The deal continues a pattern The Machine Herald has tracked across 2026: enterprise security vendors racing to extend identity and zero-trust controls to autonomous software agents whose deployment is outpacing the controls meant to govern them, as [previously reported](/article/2026-03/27-rsac-2026-reveals-an-industry-racing-to-secure-ai-agents-it-has-barely-begun-to-deploy) from RSAC 2026 and in earlier coverage of how [zero trust is being extended to non-human identities](/article/2026-03/23-zero-trust-evolves-beyond-network-perimeters-as-microsoft-the-pentagon-and-zscaler-extend-the-framework-to-ai-agents-weapons-systems-and-data-sovereignty).

## What We Know

**Deal terms.** Cisco did not publicly disclose financial terms in its announcement, [SiliconANGLE noted](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/04/cisco-buys-astrix-security-strengthen-ai-agent-discovery-governance/). [Calcalist reported](https://www.calcalistech.com/ctechnews/article/dy5obf581) the deal at approximately $400 million; [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/04/cisco-buys-astrix-security-strengthen-ai-agent-discovery-governance/) cited a separate report from The Information that placed the value closer to $300 million. [SecurityWeek also wrote](https://www.securityweek.com/cisco-moves-to-acquire-astrix-security-to-tackle-non-human-identity-risks/) that the deal is valued at approximately $400 million per the Calcalist report.

**About Astrix.** Founded in 2021 by Alon Jackson and Idan Gour, both veterans of Israel's Unit 8200 intelligence unit, [according to Calcalist](https://www.calcalistech.com/ctechnews/article/dy5obf581). The company raised a $45 million Series B in late 2024, [SecurityWeek reported](https://www.securityweek.com/cisco-moves-to-acquire-astrix-security-to-tackle-non-human-identity-risks/), bringing total funding to $85 million, [per Calcalist](https://www.calcalistech.com/ctechnews/article/dy5obf581). The Series B was led by Menlo Ventures through its Anthology Fund partnership with Anthropic, with participation from Workday Ventures, Bessemer Venture Partners, CRV, and F2 Venture Capital, [Calcalist reported](https://www.calcalistech.com/ctechnews/article/dy5obf581).

**Product scope.** Astrix's platform focuses on discovering, governing, and securing the credentials that machines and software agents use to interact with enterprise systems, including API keys, service accounts, and OAuth tokens, [according to SecurityWeek](https://www.securityweek.com/cisco-moves-to-acquire-astrix-security-to-tackle-non-human-identity-risks/). [SiliconANGLE reported](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/04/cisco-buys-astrix-security-strengthen-ai-agent-discovery-governance/) that the platform enables real-time inventory management of AI agents, MCP servers, and non-human identities with contextual threat assessment.

**Integration plan.** [SecurityWeek wrote](https://www.securityweek.com/cisco-moves-to-acquire-astrix-security-to-tackle-non-human-identity-risks/) that Cisco plans to integrate Astrix's capabilities into its broader security platform, including identity intelligence, secure access, and Duo IAM. [SiliconANGLE added](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/04/cisco-buys-astrix-security-strengthen-ai-agent-discovery-governance/) that telemetry from the combined platform is also expected to feed into Splunk SIEM environments.

**Cisco's framing.** Peter Bailey, Senior Vice President and General Manager of Cisco's Security Business Group, framed the transaction around the rise of autonomous AI agents inside enterprises, [according to SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/04/cisco-buys-astrix-security-strengthen-ai-agent-discovery-governance/). Bailey said AI agents improve productivity but create "a new and potentially dangerous attack surface," [Calcalist reported](https://www.calcalistech.com/ctechnews/article/dy5obf581). [SiliconANGLE wrote](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/04/cisco-buys-astrix-security-strengthen-ai-agent-discovery-governance/) that, citing Cisco's own AI Readiness Index data, only 24% of businesses have the ability to control AI agents with proper guardrails, and 31% of enterprises believe they are "fully capable" of securing agentic deployments.

## What We Don't Know

Cisco has not published an official deal value, leaving two competing third-party figures — Calcalist's approximately $400 million and The Information's roughly $300 million reported by SiliconANGLE — without official confirmation. Closing date and any regulatory review steps were not described in the cited reports. Specific Astrix headcount and the post-acquisition reporting structure inside Cisco's Security Business Group were also not disclosed in the sources reviewed.

The sources also do not detail whether Astrix's standalone product will continue to be sold to customers who are not on Cisco's security platform, nor what happens to Astrix's existing investor cap table beyond the high-level commitment to integrate the technology into Cisco Identity Intelligence, Cisco Secure Access, and Duo IAM, [as SecurityWeek described](https://www.securityweek.com/cisco-moves-to-acquire-astrix-security-to-tackle-non-human-identity-risks/).

## Analysis

The Astrix deal arrives roughly six weeks after RSAC 2026, where Cisco itself introduced what it called a framework for securing the "agentic workforce," as [The Machine Herald previously reported](/article/2026-03/27-rsac-2026-reveals-an-industry-racing-to-secure-ai-agents-it-has-barely-begun-to-deploy). Within that framework, Cisco committed to building protections around AI agents through identity, runtime, and incident-response layers, but its inventory of dedicated tooling for non-human-identity discovery and governance was thin. Astrix slots directly into that gap.

The deal also fits a broader vendor pattern. As [The Machine Herald reported in March](/article/2026-03/23-zero-trust-evolves-beyond-network-perimeters-as-microsoft-the-pentagon-and-zscaler-extend-the-framework-to-ai-agents-weapons-systems-and-data-sovereignty), Microsoft, Zscaler, and federal agencies have begun extending zero-trust principles to non-human identities, with industry estimates indicating that machine and non-human identities now significantly outnumber human users in many enterprises. Vendors that owned identity for human users — Cisco's Duo IAM among them — are now being asked whether they can also own identity for software agents.

The two reported deal values, $400 million and $300 million, are small relative to Cisco's recent large security and observability acquisitions, but consistent with what acquirers have paid in 2025 and 2026 for early-stage identity-security companies whose primary asset is product fit rather than current revenue. With Astrix carrying $85 million in total funding [per Calcalist](https://www.calcalistech.com/ctechnews/article/dy5obf581), either reported figure represents a meaningful return for the Series B investors who participated in late 2024.

The more interesting question is whether the acquisition tilts the still-unsettled non-human-identity category toward consolidation. Astrix's competitors — startups that have raised under a similar thesis — now face a market in which one of the largest enterprise security vendors will bundle NHI discovery and governance with identity, access, and SIEM offerings the same buyers already consume. Cisco's framing of the deal around "the agentic workforce" suggests the company expects AI agents, not just service accounts, to be the long-run driver of identity sprawl that justifies such a platform. Whether that thesis holds, and whether Astrix's discovery and governance tooling remains best-in-class once embedded inside a much larger security stack, will be visible only after the integration with Cisco Identity Intelligence, Cisco Secure Access, and Duo IAM is in customers' hands.
